platform_decisions.content: Tool interface is task-oriented ToolDef with call(), checkPermissions(), validateInput(), inputSchema, outputSchema
Default tool behaviors are fail-closed via buildTool() — missing implementations default to deny-safe
All core tools use lazy schema evaluation (lazySchema()) to defer Zod compilation until first use
Permission system is three-layer: validateInput() then checkPermissions() then global deny-rules pre-filter
Permission rules are source-scoped with 8 sources: policy > flag > project > local > user > cli > command > session
Deny rules short-circuit at tool-registry assembly — blanket denials prevent tool from reaching model entirely
File-modifying tools require pre-read validation with mtime+content TOCTOU check
Bash tool permission matching is compound-command-aware via AST parsing
Two-stage path normalization: backfillObservableInput() for hooks, then validateInput() re-expansion
Windows UNC paths blocked to prevent NTLM credential leaks
Case-insensitive path comparison prevents bypass on macOS/Windows
Bash tool parses compound commands via AST so chained commands trigger correct rules
Read-only bash checking requires ALL pipeline parts to be search/read commands
Sandbox is opt-out (default enabled) — respects user exclusion rules and dangerouslyDisableSandbox
Blocked device paths (/dev/zero, /dev/random, /dev/stdin) prevent hangs on read
Validation is deterministic and side-effect-free, returning ValidationResult
File existence validation is bidirectional with readFileState TOCTOU checks
Similar-file suggestions on ENOENT use fuzzy heuristic for UX guidance
Read-only tools declare isReadOnly()=true, isConcurrencySafe()=true, enabling parallel execution
Concurrency safety is derived from read-only status for Bash tool
Tool result sizes bounded by maxResultSizeChars — FileRead sets Infinity to avoid circular persist-read loop
Result serialization is two-stage: API-consumable ToolResultBlockParam + React UI rendering
Error messages are model-facing, preserving failure context as ToolResultBlockParam strings
Bash auto-backgrounds after 15 seconds if no progress; background spawns independent task
File edit/write tools are not concurrency-safe to prevent simultaneous edits
All six core tools use strict:true schema — Zod strictObject rejects unknown fields
Input schema is single source of truth — no duplicate validation logic
Filesystem abstracted via getFsImplementation() for testing mocks and platform-specific impls
Line-ending handling explicit: Write normalizes to LF, Read normalizes CRLF to LF
Tool availability feature-gated via Statsig — Glob/Grep disabled if ripgrep embedded in bun
Conditional tools use lazy requires to avoid circular dependencies
Tool execution traces structured metadata: command type, file extensions, permission decisions
File history tracking hooks into write operations for rollback capability
FileRead supports multiple types (text, image, PDF, notebook) with type-discriminated output
FileEdit uses quote-normalization and rejects non-unique matches (replace_all=false)
FileWrite is full-content replacement respecting explicit line endings as author intent
GlobTool results hardcap at 100 files with truncated flag
GrepTool wraps ripgrep with default 250-line limit, auto VCS exclusion, opt-in multiline
Bash results persist at 30KB threshold; stdout/stderr captured separately
ENOENT errors caught, normalized, augmented with suggestions; other errors pass through
Validation errors include errorCode integers for SDK programmatic parsing
No shell injection risk: command parsing is stateless AST, paths never interpolated into commands
No TOCTOU on edits: mtime+content check with no yields between check and write
Permissions applied at three levels: pre-validation semantic + checkPermissions policy + registry pre-filter
Agents spawned via AgentTool with explicit subagent_type parameter selecting from registry
Fork subagent inherits parent full context (system prompt, tool pool) for cache-efficient spawning
Multi-agent teams are hierarchical: single team lead + N teammates, flat roster, no nesting
Teammates spawned via explicit name parameter, making them addressable via SendMessage
Three teammate execution backends: in-process (AsyncLocalStorage), tmux, iTerm2
In-process teammates use AsyncLocalStorage for context isolation without subprocesses
Agent messages use file-based mailbox with proper-lockfile for concurrent writes
Task lifecycle: pending to in_progress to completed with blocks/blockedBy dependency tracking
Task ownership auto-assigned to current agent name when status set to in_progress
Plan mode requires explicit approval before implementation; team lead controls gate
Plan approval uses request-response messaging with typed message protocol
Worktrees provide git-native branch isolation; stores original HEAD for restoration
Worktree slug validation prevents path traversal (rejects .., absolute paths, non-alnum)
Large directories (node_modules) symlinked from main repo to worktree to avoid duplication
Session state fully restored after worktree exit (CWD, projectRoot, caches)
Agent shutdown uses request-approval protocol via SendMessage typed messages
Background tasks stoppable via TaskStopTool with abort controller
One team per leader session; second team creation fails with error
TeamDelete requires all active teammates to shut down first
Task list ID resolution: env var then in-process then tmux then leader then session
High water mark prevents task ID reuse after team reset
Concurrent swarm operations use proper-lockfile with retry backoff (30 retries, about 2.6s)
MCP server requirements block agent spawn if unmet (30s wait timeout)
Agent model supports inherit alias to adopt parent/leader model
Permission modes propagate down teammate hierarchy
Agents color-coded for UI grouping via assignTeammateColor()
Five external permission modes: default, acceptEdits, bypassPermissions, dontAsk, plan
Internal auto mode gated by TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER feature flag, stripped from external modes
Three-tier permission behavior: allow, deny, ask
Permission rules composed as toolName + ruleContent with wildcard matching
Seven rule sources with priority: policy > flag > project > local > user > cli > command > session
Rule cascade: deny wins, ask escalates allow, first-match for path rules
Managed permission rules only mode disables all user editing when enterprise policy active
Permission rule sources map to specific file locations with persistence semantics
Dangerous files always require ask: .gitconfig, .bashrc, .zshrc, .mcp.json, .claude.json
Dangerous directories always require ask: .git/, .vscode/, .idea/, .claude/
Claude config scope (settings.json, commands/, agents/, skills/) always requires ask
Skill-scoped permission suggestions prevent broad .claude/ grants
Case-insensitive path normalization for cross-platform bypass prevention
Path traversal detection rejects .. sequences with macOS symlink normalization
Working directory scope enforced — all file operations must resolve within allowed paths
Cross-platform code execution patterns stripped in auto mode (interpreters, package runners, shells, remote)
Dangerous bash permission rules stripped in auto mode (tool-level allow, interpreter prefixes)
Dangerous PowerShell patterns stripped: Invoke-Expression, Start-Process, Invoke-Command
Auto mode entry strips dangerous allow rules to stash; exit restores them
Safe tools allowlist skips classifier: FileRead, Grep, Glob, LSP, Task, Plan tools
Auto mode classifier two-stage: fast shallow check then extended reasoning if inconclusive
Pending classifier check is non-blocking — user sees allow immediately, classifier may feedback later
Sandbox wraps anthropic sandbox-runtime enforcing OS-level filesystem/network boundaries
Sandbox config converted from settings: FileRead to read allowlist, WebFetch to domain allowlist
Excluded commands skip sandbox — convenience feature, not security boundary
Sandbox disabled check: isSandboxingEnabled then dangerouslyDisable then excludedCommand then active
Permission decision reason types provide full audit trail: rule, mode, classifier, hook, safetyCheck
Five permission update operations: addRules, replaceRules, removeRules, setMode, add/removeDirectories
Permission updates persisted to settings files or kept session-only based on destination
Read permission checking validates all paths from input against readable internal paths
Write permission checking: traversal detection + case normalization + dangerous file/dir check + working dir
Sensitive paths auto-protected: .claude/settings.json, commands/, agents/, skills/, plans/
Permission request hooks run before prompting — can modify, block, or suggest auto-adds
Tools can implement permissionPromptTool for custom approval UI (e.g. FileEdit shows diffs)
Bypass permissions mode has enterprise killswitch via GrowthBook gate
Bypass disabled context modification removes mode from UI options entirely
Multi-layer security: permission rules then mode system then auto classifier then sandbox then path validation then hooks then denial tracking
Injection protection: path traversal, case-sensitivity bypass, symlink normalization, UNC paths, shell injection classifier
Denial tracking with rate limiting prevents user frustration from repeated identical prompts
Tool permission context built at startup, stored in AppState, updated reactively on settings change
Mode transitions centralized via transitionPermissionMode() — CLI, SDK, UI all behave identically
